skill.name: haskell-pro
skill.description: Expert Haskell engineer specializing in advanced type systems, pure functional design, and high-reliability software. Use PROACTIVELY for type-level programming, concurrency, and architecture guidance.
skill.summary: Expert Haskell engineer for advanced type systems, pure functional design, and high-reliability software architecture.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 7 potential issues (1 external_commands, 6 weak cryptographic patterns). All findings are false positives: the 'external_commands' detection was Markdown backticks for file paths, not shell execution. The 'weak cryptographic' detections appear to be scanner errors as no cryptographic code exists in the skill. The skill contains only documentation and instructions for Haskell programming assistance with no executable code, network operations, or security risks.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown File Reference
low_findings.description: Line 27 contains backticks used for Markdown formatting of a file path ('resources/implementation-playbook.md'), not shell command execution. The static analyzer incorrectly identified this as an external command execution pattern.

content.user_title: Master Advanced Haskell Programming with Expert Guidance
content.value_statement: Struggling with complex type systems and pure functional design in Haskell? This skill provides expert guidance on type-level programming, concurrency patterns, and building high-reliability software with strong static guarantees.
actual_capabilities: Design advanced type systems using GADTs, type families, and phantom types
Architect pure functional applications with explicit IO boundaries
Implement concurrency patterns using STM, async, and lightweight threads
Create typeclass hierarchies with law-driven development
Optimize Haskell code with strictness analysis and profiling techniques
Structure Cabal/Stack projects with proper dependency management
limitations: Cannot execute or compile Haskell code directly - provides guidance only
Does not run tests or build projects - offers patterns and examples
Cannot debug runtime errors without code context and error messages
Limited to Haskell-specific guidance - does not cover non-Haskell tooling

use_cases.title: Type-Level Programming
use_cases.description: Design sophisticated type-level abstractions that encode business logic in the type system, catching errors at compile time
use_cases.target_user: Haskell developers building type-safe APIs and domain models

use_cases.title: Concurrent Systems Design
use_cases.description: Build concurrent and parallel applications using STM, async, and lightweight threads with proper exception safety
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers implementing high-performance Haskell services

use_cases.title: Codebase Architecture Review
use_cases.description: Evaluate existing Haskell codebases for functional design patterns, type safety, and architectural improvements
use_cases.target_user: Teams maintaining or refactoring Haskell applications

prompt_templates.title: Type-Safe Domain Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a type-safe domain model for a [domain] system. Use newtypes, phantom types, or GADTs to encode invariants at the type level and prevent invalid states.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new Haskell project that needs strong compile-time guarantees

prompt_templates.title: Refactor to Pure Functions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Haskell code and refactor it to separate pure logic from effectful code. Identify where IO can be isolated and propose a function signature architecture.
prompt_templates.scenario: Improving code testability by reducing side effects

prompt_templates.title: Concurrent Design Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a concurrent [system] using Haskell's STM and async. Show how to handle shared state safely and implement exception-safe resource cleanup.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a concurrent system that requires shared state management

prompt_templates.title: Performance Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Haskell code for performance bottlenecks. Identify opportunities for strictness analysis, fusion optimizations, or better data structure choices.
prompt_templates.scenario: Profiling and optimizing a Haskell application for better performance

output_examples.input: Design a type-safe API for handling money transfers that prevents invalid states at compile time.
output_examples.output: Use newtypes to wrap Amount and Currency with phantom types to track transfer states (Pending, Completed, Failed). Implement a state machine using GADTs that only allows valid transitions. Encode account invariants in types to prevent overdrafts. Provide type signatures that make invalid transfers unrepresentable.

output_examples.input: How do I handle concurrent access to a shared cache in Haskell?
output_examples.output: Use STM (Software Transactional Memory) with TVar for atomic cache updates. Wrap cache operations in retry blocks for automatic conflict resolution. Combine with async for concurrent read/write operations. Implement exception-safe cleanup using bracket or finally. Example pattern: atomically $ do cached <- readTVar cacheVar; case cached of Just val -> return val; Nothing -> do newValue <- computeValue; writeTVar cacheVar (Just newValue); return newValue

output_examples.input: Review my Haskell code for functional design improvements.
output_examples.output: Separate pure business logic into standalone functions with explicit signatures. Isolate database and network IO using transformer stacks (ReaderT for config, ExceptT for errors). Replace partial functions with total alternatives using Maybe or Either. Introduce newtypes to distinguish between UserID and Username at the type level. Split large modules into focused single-responsibility modules.

best_practices: Start by clarifying your specific Haskell challenge - whether it is type-level design, concurrency, architecture, or performance tuning
Provide relevant code snippets, error messages, or compiler warnings for more targeted guidance
Specify your GHC version and key dependencies when requesting architecture or compatibility advice
anti_patterns: Asking for non-Haskell programming help - use a general coding skill instead
Requesting execution of arbitrary system commands or file operations outside your project context
Expecting complete application implementations without providing project requirements or constraints

faq.question: Can this skill write and compile Haskell code for me?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides expert guidance, patterns, and examples for Haskell programming. It helps you design type systems, architecture, and solutions, but you must implement and compile the code yourself using GHC or your build system.

faq.question: Should I use this skill for learning Haskell basics?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on advanced Haskell topics like type-level programming and concurrency. For Haskell fundamentals, consider a beginner-friendly resource first, then use this skill when you are ready for advanced patterns and architecture.

faq.question: What information should I provide for the best guidance?
faq.answer: Share your specific challenge, relevant code snippets, GHC version, and constraints. For architecture questions, explain your system requirements. For performance issues, provide profiler output or bottlenecks you have identified.

faq.question: Can this skill help with legacy Haskell codebases?
faq.answer: Yes, provide the code you want to refactor or improve. The skill can identify opportunities for better type safety, pure function extraction, and architectural improvements while explaining the reasoning behind changes.

faq.question: Does this skill cover web frameworks like Yesod or Servant?
faq.answer: The skill covers Haskell type systems, concurrency, and architecture principles that apply to web development. It can help design type-safe APIs and effectful code patterns, but for framework-specific questions, provide context about your framework choice.

faq.question: Can this skill help me choose between Cabal and Stack?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill covers project structure, build tools, and dependency hygiene. Describe your project requirements, team context, and constraints, and it can guide you toward the appropriate tooling choice with rationale.
